clinical_coherence.notes: 31F African American with migraine, anxiety, and episodic neurological symptoms. PMH and presentation are structurally very similar to MS-RR-P01 (same PMH pattern: migraine, anxiety, iron deficiency anemia, prior vertigo). The clinical exam shows subtle but real findings (mild gaze-evoked nystagmus, subtle hyperreflexia). The overall clinical picture is plausible but the extremely close similarity to P01 is notable.

tool_output_consistency.notes: Initial MRI shows lesions that create genuine ambiguity between migraine and MS. CSF shows only 2 OCBs (borderline). VEP is normal. Follow-up MRI at 6 months shows new enhancing periventricular lesion with Dawson finger morphology, confirming DIT. The progression from ambiguity to diagnostic clarity is well-designed.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD G35 correct. Differential appropriately includes SLE given maternal aunt with lupus and low-titer ANA. Key reasoning about SLE vs MS distinction is clinically sound. The ground truth notes that anti-dsDNA, complement levels, and full lupus workup are needed.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Genuine diagnostic puzzle: borderline CSF (2 OCBs), equivocal initial MRI, family history of SLE creating an additional competing diagnosis. African American demographic raises the pretest probability for both SLE and more aggressive MS.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 31F African American with family history of SLE is highly plausible. The SLE differential is epidemiologically appropriate for this demographic.

educational_value.notes: Good teaching case for SLE vs MS differential, which is an important clinical distinction. However, the overall structure is very similar to P01, reducing additive educational value within the set.

issues_found: PMH pattern (migraine, anxiety, iron deficiency, prior vertigo) is nearly identical to MS-RR-P01
Case structure closely mirrors P01, limiting diversity in the puzzle set
strengths: SLE as a competing diagnosis is clinically important and well-integrated
African American demographic appropriately raises SLE consideration
Borderline CSF findings create genuine diagnostic uncertainty
Well-calibrated evolution from ambiguity to diagnosis on follow-up MRI
